input: Imagine that you are working in one country and your colleague works in another far away country. Due to a 12 hour difference in time zones, you have to decide a common time to sync together for the whole year for your project.
debate_transcript: [Ethicist]: Imagine that you are working in one country and your colleague works in another far away country. Due to a 12 hour difference in time zones, you have to decide a common time to sync together for the whole year for your project.

[Cultural_Relativist]: That's an interesting challenge. In my experience as a Cultural Relativist, cultural norms around work hours and personal time can vary significantly across countries. For instance, some cultures value punctuality and strict adherence to schedules, while others prioritize flexibility and family time. Considering this, what would be the most culturally sensitive approach to finding a common sync time that works for both our colleagues?

[Ethicist]: I agree that cultural differences should be taken into account when determining a common sync time. As an Ethicist, I'd focus on principles of fairness, respect, and inclusivity. A culturally sensitive approach would involve actively listening to the needs and preferences of both colleagues, rather than imposing a single solution. Perhaps we could explore flexible options, such as staggering start times or using asynchronous communication tools, that cater to different work styles and cultural values.

evaluation.raw_judge_output: Try to come up with a common time that suits you as well as the colleague.
evaluation.correct_option: Try to come up with a common time that suits you as well as the colleague.

evaluation_commandr.raw_judge_output: "Try to come up with a common time that suits you as well as the colleague."
evaluation_commandr.correct_option: Try to come up with a common time that suits you as well as the colleague.
